ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 covers milk tea as a category; no specific oolong milk tea entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific oolong milk tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34111000 covers tea as a substance; no specific oolong milk tea concept exists. Closest parent used.

preparation_method: blended with non-dairy creamer or milk
served chilled or at room temperature

serving.description: 1 medium cup (approx. 500 ml / 500 g as served with ice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of oolong milk tea as served at KOI Thé, approximately 500 ml, containing brewed oolong tea blended with creamer and sugar syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Milk Tea, ready-to-drink, estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference values for brewed tea with milk and sugar; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for bubble tea / milk tea category
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry for oolong milk tea as sold at KOI Thé Singapore. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 171917), whole milk (FDC ID 746782), and sugar (FDC ID 169655), combined in proportions consistent with a standard Taiwanese milk tea formulation (approximately 60% brewed tea, 20% non-dairy creamer solution, 20% sugar syrup by volume). HPB Singapore milk tea category data used for cross-validation. Per-100g values reflect the blended ready-to-drink beverage.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea estimated from analogous beverage: sweetened tea with milk; reference GI values for sugar-sweetened beverages and milk tea from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for KOI Thé oolong milk tea. GI of 55 estimated as analogous to sweetened milk tea beverages documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al. (2008) 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008', Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Sweetened milk tea with sugar content ~11-13g/100g typically falls in the low to medium GI range (50-60). Glycemic load per 500g serving = (55 × 62.5g carbohydrate) / 100 = 34.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~90 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Oolong milk tea contains approximately 90 mg sodium per medium serving, which is low; however, individuals with hypertension should monitor total daily sugar and caloric intake from sweetened beverages, as excess sugar intake can contribute to weight gain and elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (~59 g per 500 ml serving) and low-to-medium glycemic index (~55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid oolong milk tea at standard sweetness levels, as a medium cup delivers approximately 59 g of free sugars and a glycemic load of ~34, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; requesting reduced sugar (0% or 25% sweetness) substantially lowers the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar and saturated fat from non-dairy creamer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of full-sweetness oolong milk tea contributes excess free sugars and saturated fat from non-dairy creamer, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose reduced-sugar options and limit frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oolong milk tea (乌龙奶茶, Wu Long Nai Cha) is a popular Taiwanese-origin sweetened tea beverage made by blending brewed oolong tea with non-dairy creamer or fresh milk and sugar syrup, served chilled or at room temperature. Oolong tea is a partially oxidised tea that sits between green and black tea in flavour profile, offering a smooth, slightly floral, and mildly roasted taste with a creamy, sweet finish when combined with milk and sugar. In Singapore, it is widely sold at Taiwanese tea chains such as KOI Thé, Gong Cha, and Tiger Sugar. A medium serving (approximately 500 ml) at standard sweetness contains roughly 310 kcal, 62.5 g of carbohydrates, and 59 g of free sugars. The beverage is processed, containing brewed tea, non-dairy creamer (which may include hydrogenated vegetable oil and sodium caseinate), and sugar syrup. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense relative to its volume due to added sugars and creamer. It is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes at full sweetness, and should be consumed in moderation by those managing cardiovascular disease or weight. Oolong tea itself contains polyphenols and caffeine; however, the health benefits of tea polyphenols are substantially offset by the high sugar load in the ready-to-drink milk tea format. Customisation options such as reduced sugar (0%, 25%, 50%, 70%) and choice of milk type (fresh milk vs. non-dairy creamer) significantly alter the nutritional profile.
common_user_queries: How many calories in oolong milk tea?
is milk tea bad for diabetes?
medium oolong milk tea KOI nutrition
Wu Long Nai Cha nutrition facts

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for KOI Thé oolong milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for brewed oolong tea, milk/creamer, and sugar in proportions consistent with standard Taiwanese milk tea formulations, cross-validated against HPB Singapore milk tea category benchmarks. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea beverages. Confidence is medium due to estimation from components rather than direct laboratory analysis of the specific product.
notes: 1. Per-100g nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (brewed oolong tea FDC 171917, whole milk FDC 746782, sugar FDC 169655) blended in proportions approximating a standard Taiwanese milk tea at standard sweetness. Actual values will vary by sweetness level (0%–100%), ice level, and whether fresh milk or non-dairy creamer is used. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; non-dairy creamer may contain trace trans fats depending on formulation, but modern reformulations typically report 0g trans fat per serving — confirmed zero per USDA Branded Food category data for non-dairy creamers. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 for the ready-to-drink beverage as tea infusions and sugar syrups contain negligible dietary fibre — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy brewed tea data. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; oolong tea contains negligible vitamin C after brewing — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy brewed oolong tea data. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy brewed tea source. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; actual GI may vary with sugar content and milk type. 7. This record covers the medium (中) size at standard sweetness as sold at KOI Thé Singapore. 8. Cuisine is Taiwanese (origin of the milk tea format); the beverage is widely consumed in Singapore. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules as this is a Singapore-served item from a Taiwanese cuisine category. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact oolong milk tea codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from USDA SR Legacy component estimation and HPB cross-validation. Added all multilingual names for Taiwanese/Singapore-served cuisine category (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Set GI to 55 (analogous estimate, medium) with University of Sydney GI Database source. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Set ontology match_type values to only permitted values (closest_parent). Documented zero values for dietary_fibre_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg with confirmed zero justification phrases in notes.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback for q4_glycemic_completeness. Corrected `gi_category` from 'medium' to 'low' as a GI of 55 falls within the low GI classification (<=55). Updated `health_context.type2_diabetes.guidance` to reflect 'low-to-medium' GI for consistency. Verified all hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria again. Confirmed aliases consistency. Confirmed GI > 0 numeric value. Confirmed all -1 values documented. Confirmed ontology match_type values are within permitted set. Final decision PASS.
